steps.title: Substrate recognition in S1–S4 pockets
steps.description: Mpro recognises the Leu-Gln↓(Ser/Ala/Gly) motif. Gln P1 hydrogen-bonds to His163, Glu166 and Phe140 in the S1 subsite — the Gln specificity is absolute and is what distinguishes Mpro from host cysteine proteases. Hydrophobic P2 (usually Leu) inserts into a pocket lined by His41, Met49 and Tyr54.
steps.notes: Mpro is only catalytically active as a homodimer; the N-terminal residues (the 'N-finger') of each protomer organise the S1 pocket of the other.

steps.title: Thiolate formation — His41 deprotonates Cys145
steps.description: In the resting enzyme Cys145 (pKa ~8) and His41 (pKa ~6) exist as an ion-pair (thiolate-imidazolium) within the active site. Substrate binding stabilises this reactive form.

steps.title: Nucleophilic attack → tetrahedral intermediate
steps.description: The Cys145 thiolate attacks the scissile carbonyl of Gln P1. The resulting tetrahedral oxyanion is stabilised by the oxyanion hole (backbone NH of Gly143, Ser144, Cys145).

steps.title: Collapse → acyl-enzyme (thioester) + amine product
steps.description: The oxyanion collapses. His41 (now imidazolium) donates its proton to the departing amine nitrogen. The C-terminal peptide diffuses out. The N-terminal portion of the substrate remains as a thioester covalently bound to Cys145 — the acyl-enzyme intermediate.

steps.title: Water attacks acyl-enzyme
steps.description: A water molecule, activated by His41 (general base again), attacks the thioester carbonyl. A second tetrahedral intermediate is stabilised by the oxyanion hole.

steps.title: Acyl-enzyme hydrolysis — N-terminal product released
steps.description: The second oxyanion collapses. His41+ donates its proton to the Cys145 thiolate, breaking the C–S bond. The N-terminal peptide (carboxylic acid) leaves, and the free dimer is ready for the next substrate.
steps.notes: Nirmatrelvir (active ingredient of Paxlovid) traps step 3 as a stable covalent adduct — a nitrile warhead forms a thioimidate with Cys145 that mimics the tetrahedral transition state.

references.citation: Jin Z et al. Structure of Mpro from SARS-CoV-2 and discovery of its inhibitors. Nature 582:289–293 (2020).
Owen DR et al. An oral SARS-CoV-2 Mpro inhibitor clinical candidate for the treatment of COVID-19. Science 374:1586–1593 (2021).
Anand K et al. Coronavirus main proteinase (3CLpro) structure: basis for design of anti-SARS drugs. Science 300:1763–1767 (2003).
